# Nutrition across the life cycle

> *The healthy plate is a **baseline**, not a fixed prescription. What a body needs shifts
> predictably from the womb to old age — and anticipating those shifts is a large part of giving
> nutrition advice that actually fits the person in front of you.*

## Nutrition is dynamic

You already know the shape of a healthy plate — the ¼–¼–½ baseline from Part I. This chapter adds
the dimension a single plate can't capture: **time**. A pregnant woman, a six-month-old infant, a
growing teenager and a frail 80-year-old are not eating wrong versions of one diet; they have
genuinely different needs, and "one plate fits all" quietly fails each of them. (How to read and
compare dietary guidance in depth comes later, when we turn to advising the healthy.)

The task here is **anticipation and recognition** — to know which nutrients or feeding principles
define each stage, and to spot when a life-stage need has become a clinical problem — not to write
the detailed therapeutic plan (that is dietitian and later-year work). Step along the arc below to
see the shape of it, then we walk each stage in turn.

<div data-island="life-stages" data-src="/diagrams/life-stages.yml"></div>

## Pregnancy and lactation

**Quality far more than quantity.** The oldest myth here is "eating for two." In fact energy needs
rise only **modestly** and mainly in the later trimesters — on the order of an extra small meal a
day, not a doubling [@whoAntenatal2016]. What matters far more is a handful of specific
**micronutrients**:

- **Folate** — periconceptional **folic acid** (around 400 µg/day, ideally started *before*
  conception) sharply reduces the risk of **neural tube defects** such as spina bifida. Because the
  neural tube closes in the first weeks — often before a pregnancy is confirmed — the timing is the
  whole point [@whoAntenatal2016].
- **Iron** — requirements rise substantially as maternal blood volume expands and the fetus builds
  its own stores; iron-deficiency anaemia is common in pregnancy.
- **Iodine** — needed for the thyroid hormone that drives **fetal brain development**; the early
  fetus depends entirely on the mother's supply.
- **Calcium and vitamin D** — important for the developing fetal skeleton. (Interestingly, the
  *requirement* doesn't rise much, because the gut simply absorbs dietary calcium far more
  efficiently in pregnancy — but adequate intake still matters.)

**Food safety** joins the picture: avoid **alcohol** entirely (no known safe amount), **limit
high-mercury fish** (shark, swordfish, marlin), and avoid **unpasteurised dairy and other
high-listeria foods**, which can cause miscarriage or stillbirth [@whoAntenatal2016]. Detailed
supplementation regimens are covered where the *Micronutrients, Hydration & Electrolytes* chapter
left off.

**Lactation flips the energy message.** Making milk is the one life stage where energy needs
genuinely *do* climb — on the order of an extra **450–500 kcal a day**, with higher fluid needs too.
So a breastfeeding mother really is, near enough, eating for two — the one time that phrase earns its
keep.

### Gestational diabetes: why Singapore screens everyone

Singapore's defining antenatal nutrition issue is **gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)** — glucose
intolerance first recognised in pregnancy. It is strikingly common locally: the **GUSTO** birth
cohort found GDM in roughly **one in five** pregnancies [@gusto_gdm]. (Treat that "~18.9%" as
cohort- and criteria-dependent — it came from one Singapore cohort using older 1999 WHO
diagnostic criteria — not as a fixed national rate.)

The load-bearing lesson is *how* we screen. GUSTO showed that if Singapore had tested only
**high-risk** women — the older, heavier, family-history approach used in some countries —
it would have **missed about half** of all GDM cases, because risk factors predict GDM poorly in
Asian populations [@gusto_gdm]. So Singapore screens **universally**: every pregnancy is offered a
**75 g oral glucose tolerance test at 24–28 weeks**, whatever the risk profile [@aceGDM2018].

::: {.callout-note}
## The numbers, for recognition (not recall)
A positive test is any one of: fasting glucose **≥ 5.1**, 1-hour **≥ 10.0**, or 2-hour **≥ 8.5
mmol/L** (the IADPSG thresholds) [@aceGDM2018]. You don't memorise these numbers — the point
to carry is the *pathway*: **screen everyone at 24–28 weeks**, and know that a woman with GDM needs
a **postpartum glucose test** and lifelong higher vigilance, because GDM strongly predicts future
**type 2 diabetes**. That link is exactly why GDM sits inside Singapore's *War on Diabetes*
[@mohWarOnDiabetes].
:::

First-line management is dietary — lower-glycaemic local swaps and spreading carbohydrate through
the day — delivered by a **team** (obstetrician, dietitian, diabetes nurse). Insulin and glucose
targets are the diabetes chapter's territory, not this one.

## Infancy

**Start with breast milk.** WHO and HPB recommend **exclusive breastfeeding for about the first six
months** — breast milk alone, no water or other foods — for its ideal nutrition, immune protection
and bonding, then **complementary foods from around six months** (not before four, not delayed much
past six), with breastfeeding continued to two years [@whoBreastfeeding; @smjFeeding2023]. Around
six months, an infant's own **iron** stores run low, so the first solid foods should be
**iron-rich**, and there is no need for added salt or sugar.

::: {.callout-warning}
## "~6 months" is the recommendation — with an honest caveat
Exclusive breastfeeding to six months is the standing guideline, but it is genuinely debated: some
evidence suggests introducing allergenic foods and iron-rich solids in the **4–6 month** window may
help prevent allergy and iron deficiency. Teach the recommendation, and hold the nuance — this is a
live area, not settled dogma [@smjFeeding2023].
:::

## Childhood and adolescence

Through childhood, steady growth calls for **adequate energy and nutrient density**, plenty of
**variety**, and — because habits set early — a genuine effort to **limit free sugars** (for teeth
and for the long run). Family meals that model good choices do more than any rule.

**Adolescence raises the stakes.** The growth spurt and — critically — the **peak-bone-mass window**
push several needs to their lifetime highest:

- **Calcium and vitamin D** — most of the skeleton you carry for life is laid down by the late
  teens, so **adolescence has the highest calcium needs of any age**. Miss this window and it
  cannot be fully recovered later (a thread the bone-health and menopause chapters pick up).
- **Iron** — needs rise with growth, and **menstruating girls** lose iron every cycle, making them
  a classic deficiency group.
- **Energy and protein** — to fuel the spurt.

Adolescence is also a **window of vulnerability**: for micronutrient deficiency, for emerging
diet-related disease, and for **disordered eating** — restrictive dieting and body-image pressure
commonly begin here, a do-no-harm flag the *Assessing Diet & Talking to Patients* chapter takes
further.

## Older age

Old age flips the usual intuition. Energy needs **fall** (less activity, less muscle), but the need
for **nutrients does not** — so food has to become **more nutrient-dense per calorie**, not less.
The single most counter-intuitive point:

- **Protein needs go *up*, not down.** To defend muscle against **sarcopenia** and frailty, healthy
  older adults are advised roughly **1.0–1.2 g/kg/day** — above the ~0.8 g/kg of a younger adult
  [@protAge2013]. Under-eating protein is one of the commonest, most correctable drivers of decline.
- **Calcium and vitamin D** to slow bone loss; **vitamin B12**, because its absorption falls with
  age; and enough **fluid and fibre** to counter constipation.

But the deeper problem in older age is often not *what* to eat but *whether* they eat at all.
**Appetite fades, teeth and swallowing fail, and — powerfully — people eat less when they eat
alone.** Social isolation is a genuine red flag. When an older person is losing weight without
trying, that is no longer a life-stage need but a sign of **undernutrition** — recognised, screened
and managed as its own topic in the *Undernutrition & Malnutrition* chapter.

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out & 🍳 healthy kitchen: feed the person you have
For a frail senior, the win is almost never a "diet food" — it is **fortifying the familiar**. Add
an egg, some fish, minced meat or *tau kwa* to the porridge or *mee sua*; choose the **yong tau foo**
with more protein pieces; offer small, frequent meals and softer textures for tired teeth. For a
mum-to-be with GDM, keep the meals she loves but shift the staple lower-GI (brown rice, wholegrain
*bee hoon*) and swap the sweet drink for water or a Nutri-Grade A/B choice. Small, respectful
adjustments to real food beat an unfamiliar regimen every time.
:::

## The Singapore picture

Each stage has a local anchor. HPB publishes **Dietary Guidelines for Older Adults** — Singapore's
first seniors' guidelines — leading with nutrient density, more protein, calcium-rich foods, softer
textures and more fluids [@hpbSeniors]. Infant feeding follows WHO, reinforced by the local
**consensus statements** on feeding infants and young children [@smjFeeding2023]. And GDM ties
life-cycle nutrition directly to national policy: universal screening exists precisely because the
local evidence showed selective screening fails Asian women [@gusto_gdm; @aceGDM2018]. Layered on
top are the population gaps the National Nutrition Survey keeps flagging — too little wholegrain,
too much sodium — which bite hardest exactly where reserves are thinnest, in pregnancy and old age
[@mohNNS2022].

## So — is there one plate for everyone?

No — and that is the point. My Healthy Plate is the **baseline**, and life stage is the dial that
turns it: more folate and iron and a glucose test in pregnancy; breast milk then iron-rich solids in
infancy; calcium and iron through the bone-building teens; more protein and more density, eaten in
company, in old age. Your job now is to **anticipate** the stage-defining need and **recognise** when
a normal shift has tipped into a clinical problem — then advise simply, and refer when it runs deeper
than everyday guidance [@crowley2019].

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/life-cycle-nutrition.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: Mrs Devi, 26 weeks pregnant

Mrs Devi attends a routine antenatal visit. She has a raised BMI and a family history of diabetes,
and wonders whether she needs "to eat for two" and whether the glucose test her friend mentioned
really applies to her. Work through her nutritional needs and Singapore's screening pathway — and
notice where your role ends and the team's begins. Your choices branch the case.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/mrs-devi-gdm.case.yml"></div>
